source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Recognise that light is needed in order to see things and that dark is the absence of light; notice that light is reflected from surfaces; recognise that shadows are formed when light from a light source is blocked by an opaque object; find patterns in the way that the size of shadows change; and recognise that light from the sun can be dangerous.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack develops a simple source-object-eye model, uses virtual reflection and shadow investigations to identify patterns, and embeds explicit sun-safety boundaries without asking learners to look at bright light.

objective.child_goal: I can explain how light helps us see, how surfaces reflect it and how shadows form and change.
objective.statement: Use observations and simple models to explain seeing, reflection, shadow formation and shadow-size patterns while following sun-safety rules.
misconceptions: Thinks eyes send out light or that objects can be seen in complete darkness without light reaching the eye.
Treats darkness as a substance that covers objects rather than the absence of light.
Thinks only mirrors reflect light or that a reflection is light stored inside a shiny surface.
Thinks a shadow is a dark copy, can form without a light source, or changes size because the object itself changes.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Something that gives out its own light.
vocabulary.audio_script: A light source produces light, such as the Sun, a lamp or a flame.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A lamp icon with arrows travelling away from it.

vocabulary.audio_script: When light reflects, it bounces from a surface and can travel towards our eyes.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A light arrow reaching a surface and leaving in a new direction.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Does not let light pass through.
vocabulary.audio_script: An opaque object blocks light and can make a shadow.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A solid object stopping a beam before a screen.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A darker area where an opaque object blocks light.
vocabulary.audio_script: A shadow forms where light cannot reach because an object blocks it.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A source, blocking object and dark region on a screen.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort each item: does it make its own light, or can it only be seen when light reflects from it?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Distinguish sources from illuminated or reflective objects before modelling vision.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The Sun and a switched-on lamp are sources. The Moon and a road sign reflect light.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Item cards move into MAKES LIGHT and REFLECTS LIGHT columns with explanation icons.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: In a dark cave, switch on a lamp and trace the path that lets the explorer see the map.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Build a source-to-object-to-eye model and define darkness as insufficient light.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Light travels from the lamp to the map, reflects from the map and enters the eye.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Numbered arrows connect lamp, map and eye; darkness is shown by absent arrows rather than black material.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Compare foil, white card and dark fabric. All reflect some light, but the amount and direction we observe are different.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Prevent mirror-only reflection and connect claims to observed brightness.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A mirror gives a clear directed reflection, but many ordinary surfaces also reflect light into our eyes.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A virtual torch illuminates three surfaces while a sensor records relative reflected-light bars.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Move one part of the source-object-screen setup, measure the shadow and record what changed.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Investigate shadow formation and size through one-variable comparisons.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Keep two positions the same. Change one distance, measure the shadow and describe the pattern.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A safe virtual lamp, opaque shape, screen, ruler and results table share a fixed baseline.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: A transparent sheet and an opaque card are placed in the light path. Which makes the clearer shadow, and why?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check that shadow explanation uses blocked light, material behaviour and a source.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A clearer shadow forms when more light is blocked from reaching the screen.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Side-by-side light paths show transmission through transparent material and blocking by opaque card.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion how to investigate sunlight safely without ever looking at the Sun.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Gather safety reasoning and transfer light knowledge to responsible observation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Use shadows, reflected-light observations or adult-approved instruments. Never look directly at the Sun, even through a camera, lens or dark material.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Safe choices receive a shield icon; direct viewing and improvised filters remain disabled and clearly labelled unsafe.

manipulatives.purpose: Construct source-object-eye and source-surface-eye paths without implying that eyes emit light.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners place or keyboard-select numbered arrows, toggle a source and compare what can be seen when paths are complete or interrupted.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Arrows have spoken start/end labels and numbered text equivalents; a linear statement builder replaces spatial placement; brightness never flashes; low-vision mode uses thick high-contrast paths; and no activity asks the learner to face a real bright source.

manipulatives.purpose: Run controlled virtual tests of surface reflection, material blocking and shadow size.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners change one variable with sliders or stepper buttons, measure a reflected-light bar or shadow length and add observations to a table before choosing a pattern statement.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: All sliders have numeric entry and arrow-key steps; graphs have table equivalents; shadow edges use texture and outline as well as contrast; motion can be paused; and reduced-motion mode shows labelled before/after setups.

practice.interleaving_notes: Secure source/object/eye pathways before mixing reflection and shadows; introduce shadow-size pattern items only after learners can identify the source, blocker and receiving surface.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain seeing as light travelling from a source, reflecting from an object and entering the eye.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: safe source + visible object + observer + ordered path choices + source-off comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use arrows as a simple model, not a claim that light moves only in single narrow rays; avoid anthropomorphic eye beams.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Distinguish light sources, reflectors and situations with too little light to see.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: item or scene + source state + visibility observation + choose/explain source, reflector or absence of light
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not imply all low-light conditions are complete darkness; language should distinguish no light from not enough light for the task.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare reflected light from varied surfaces and link observations to visibility.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: constant virtual source + two or three surfaces + sensor/brightness observation + evidence-based comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid absolute rankings unsupported by item data; specify surface condition and use relative observations rather than invented precision.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict, measure and explain shadow formation and size when source, object or screen distance changes.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: source-object-screen setup + one changed distance or material + measurement table + pattern statement + causal explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Change one variable at a time, state the chosen geometry clearly and verify every generated measurement follows the model.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Retain light-path, reflection, shadow and sun-safety knowledge through short mixed review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review queue weighted to prior errors + diagram or observation + one evidence choice + safe-action check where relevant
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Never instruct direct Sun viewing, improvised solar filters, lasers, flames or unsupervised electrical investigations.

question_variants.explanation: Indirect daylight observations do not require looking at the Sun. Eye safety is part of the method, not an optional extra.

feedback.correct: Your choice matches the light evidence and keeps the investigation safe.
feedback.repair: Remove the unsafe action and choose indirect evidence that keeps every bright source away from eyes.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks eyes send out light or that objects can be seen in complete darkness without light reaching the eye.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner draws arrows from the eye to the object or predicts unchanged visibility when every source is off.
repair_sequence: Turn the model source off and observe that the object is not visible.
Turn it on and order source to object to eye.
Interrupt each part of the path and explain the visibility change.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner constructs a new source-object-eye path and explains why the eye is a receiver, not the source.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats darkness as a substance that covers objects rather than the absence of light.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner describes darkness moving into the space or being pushed away as material.
repair_sequence: Compare the same scene with the source off and on.
Represent only the presence or absence of light-path arrows.
Complete the sentence: it is dark because not enough light...
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains a fresh dark scene as having no or insufficient light reaching the eye.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks only mirrors reflect light or that a reflection is light stored inside a shiny surface.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner classifies ordinary visible surfaces as non-reflective or says a mirror keeps light.
repair_sequence: Observe an ordinary card under a source and explain how it becomes visible.
Compare reflected-light observations from several surfaces under the same source.
Trace light arriving and leaving each surface rather than remaining inside it.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner states that varied surfaces reflect light and supports a comparison with observed evidence.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a shadow is a dark copy, can form without a light source, or changes size because the object itself changes.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner predicts a shadow with the source off, treats transparent material like opaque material, or says the moved object grew.
repair_sequence: Identify source, blocking object and receiving surface.
Trace where light is blocked and where it still reaches the screen.
Change one distance, measure before and after, and confirm the object's size is unchanged.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner predicts and explains a new shadow change using blocked light and the changed distance.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm dark-grey lab rather than pure black, no flashing sources, no sudden brightness changes and no ambient hum by default.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace travelling light arrows and changing shadows with numbered static path panels and labelled before/after measurements.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate source state, path order, material and observation; offer replay without using spatial language alone.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Pair each scientific term with a diagram and short definition, keep one claim per sentence and leave observation tables visible during explanation.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Use fixed SOURCE-OBJECT-EYE and SOURCE-BLOCKER-SCREEN routines, changing only one variable per investigation.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, allow pause and step controls, preserve measurements, and provide side-by-side comparisons and data-table alternatives.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat predictions as testable ideas, retain correct parts of a model during repair and do not reward speed or public performance.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach source, reflect, eye, opaque, transparent, block, shadow and safety with icons, gestures and sentence frames.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires at least 80% across seeing, reflection and shadow formation with safe choices. Secure requires 90% across evidence-based surface comparisons and controlled shadow-size patterns on two retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can explain that light enters our eyes, compare reflection from surfaces, describe how shadows form and investigate sunlight safely.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Stores path order, source state, surface observation, material blocking, changed variable, shadow measurement and safety choice separately so a correct label does not hide an insecure model.
next_actions: Route eye-beam models to source-object-eye reconstruction.
Route mirror-only claims to controlled surface comparison.
Repeat shadow patterns with one changed variable and explicit measurements.
Keep all real sunlight work adult-led and limited to indirect observations such as shadows.

qa.notes: Review-stage Year 3 light pack with a deterministic 220-item pilot bank and four preserved curated variants. Progression covers light sources, source-object-eye paths, darkness as insufficient light, reflection from varied surfaces, opacity, shadow formation and size, one-variable fair tests, indirect sunlight observation and eye safety. Generated candidates include SEND, dyslexia, visual, tactile and text alternatives, supported interactions, low-pressure feedback and explicit safety boundaries. Independent science, teacher, accessibility, safeguarding and renderer review remain required before promotion.
